claim_chain: RNNs trained to control upper limb models show savings when learning multiple force fields
Savings is enhanced when dimensionality of control network is higher
A uniform shift in preparatory activity persists after washout
This residual trace does not interfere with null-field behavior but facilitates re-learning
Perturbing activity along the uniform shift causally modulates savings
High-dimensional neural state space enables encoding of multiple motor memories without interference

typical_pattern: State main finding with supporting detail
Cite compatible empirical or computational work

likely_priorities: Biological plausibility of RNN training methods
Generalizability across different network architectures and learning rules
Mechanistic explanation of how error signals activate residual traces
Comparison with alternative theoretical accounts of savings
likely_attacks: RNNs trained with backpropagation may not reflect biological learning mechanisms
Lack of explicit contextual cues differs from primate motor learning paradigms
Perturbation experiments are not feasible in biological networks
Results may be specific to the MotorNet framework and training approach
Unclear how findings generalize to reinforcement learning or FORCE algorithm approaches
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly acknowledges that 'The present results are based on RNNs trained in an error-based approach using backpropagation through time' and states 'An interesting avenue for future work is to determine how the present results may or may not generalize to different neural network architectures and learning rules.' It also notes that perturbations 'is possible in a computational model but is not presently feasible in a biological neural network.' The discussion extensively positions findings relative to empirical work and acknowledges that 'In our RNNs no such contextual signal was provided' unlike primate studies, raising the question of mechanism.

examples.quoted_phrase: We have shown here that RNNs trained to control a computational model of the upper limb show behavioral signatures of savings
We interpret this residual hidden unit activity as a neural trace of the initial learning that remains after washout
These results provide evidence of a causal link between the identified neural trace of learning that remains after washout and subsequent savings
Our results are compatible with the proposal
This implies that multiple motor memories can be encoded in neural subspaces so that they do not interfere with each other

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, author names, or invented numbers appear in the output. Reviewer preferences are inferred solely from the paper's own discussion of limitations and future work.
